titleEn: Opinion | Why agricultural robots may never become much cheaper
titleZh: 农业机器人价格为何可能永远不会大幅下降
summaryZh: 本文从经济学角度挑战了“农业机器人会像GPS、无人机一样越来越便宜”的普遍预期。作者指出，机器人定价并非由制造成本决定，而是由其创造的农场经济价值决定。随着劳动力短缺加剧和除草剂退出市场，机器人可能成为少数可行方案，其价格将更紧密地绑定于替代劳动力和化学品带来的收益，而非硬件成本。这一观点对智慧畜牧领域的自动化设备采购决策具有重要参考意义。
translationZh:
如今，在田间演示现场观看自主除草机器人时，大多数人都会预期一件事：几年后这些机器会（大幅）降价。毕竟，GPS导航、无人机、摄像头和计算机都经历过这样的过程。更多的制造商、更大的产量和更便宜的电子元件通常会导致价格下降。

但我已不再确信农业机器人会重蹈覆辙。事实上，有充分理由表明自主除草机可能不会随时间推移而显著降价。在荷兰最近的一次田间演示中，瓦赫宁根大学研究中心的Joost Rijk提供了一个有趣的视角。他的推理出奇地简单。制造商不能将机器人定价高于种植者经济上可承受的水平。如果定价过高，销量就会消失。但定价过低也毫无意义，因为他们等于白白放弃利润。

**农场上的价值**

换句话说，机器人的售价并非由其制造成本决定，而是由其在农场上创造的价值决定。这是一种看待机械设备的根本不同方式。传统上，种植者主要根据购买价格比较机器。然而，对于机器人，讨论正在转向经济价值。机器人能替代多少劳动力？找到可靠工人变得有多困难？它能减少多少除草剂？不使用化学除草，作物表现是否更好？一名操作员能否同时监管多台机器人？也许最重要的是：在分秒必争的短暂作业窗口期，机器能否可靠运行？

这些问题使得计算商业案例比以往任何时候都更加复杂，也使得识别“最佳购买”变得困难得多。在一家农场经济上不划算的机器人，可能在另一家农场带来极佳回报。一些制造商公开承认这一现实。在同一活动中，荷兰制造商Andela Techniek的创始人Bas Andela完美总结道：“不要问机器人多少钱。要问它能为种植者赚多少钱。”

**机器人真正的竞争对手是什么？**

也许这才是更有趣的问题。如今，许多种植者仍将除草机器人比作喷雾器、机械锄或季节性劳动力。但如果更多除草剂退出市场呢？或者劳动力短缺持续恶化呢？比较将完全改变。机器人不再需要在价格上与化学除草竞争。相反，它可能成为少数几个实际可行的解决方案之一。这彻底改变了定价讨论。

未来十年，农业机器人无疑会变得更快、更智能、更可靠。但这并不自动意味着它们会更便宜。正如一台300马力的拖拉机并非仅根据其含钢量定价一样，机器人将越来越多地根据其在农场上带来的经济效益来估值。

然而，有一个重要区别。许多农民对特定拖拉机品牌有强烈的情感联系。多年的经验、家族历史、经销商关系和品牌忠诚度都影响购买决策。就像汽车行业一样，客户往往愿意为他们信赖的品牌支付更多。我不确定农业机器人是否也会如此。农民可能仍会爱上拖拉机引擎盖的颜色。但谈到机器人，我猜想他们会爱上商业案例本身。
featuredReason: 本文颠覆了“技术越成熟价格越低”的惯性思维，从经济价值定价角度切入，对智慧畜牧领域自动化设备的投资回报分析具有直接启发意义，尤其适用于评估机器人替代人工和化学品的真实价值。
contentFull:
Opinion | Why agricultural robots may never become much cheaper

 13-07 | Crop solutions | Expert opinion

 

 Geert Hekkert Chief editor of Future Farming

The Andela Robot Weeder carries a price tag of approximately €800,000 (US$961,000) in the Future Farming Buying Guide. But is that price driven by the cost of manufacturing the robot, or by the value it delivers on the farm? Photo: Jan Willem Schouten.

Watch an autonomous weeding robot at a field demonstration today and most people expect one thing: in a few years these machines will be (much) cheaper. After all, that is what happened with GPS guidance, drones, cameras and computers. More manufacturers, larger production volumes and cheaper electronics usually lead to lower prices.

But I'm no longer convinced the same will happen with agricultural robots. In fact, there are good reasons why autonomous weeders may not become significantly cheaper over time. During a recent field demonstration in the Netherlands, Joost Rijk of Wageningen University & Research offered an interesting perspective. His reasoning is surprisingly simple. Manufacturers cannot price a robot above what growers can economically justify. If they do, sales disappear. But pricing it much lower also makes little sense, because they would simply give away profit.

Value on the farm

In other words, the selling price of a robot is not determined by what it costs to build. It is determined by the value it creates on the farm. That is a fundamentally different way of looking at machinery. Traditionally, growers compare machines mainly on purchase price. With robots, however, the discussion is shifting towards economic value. How much labour does the robot replace? How difficult has it become to find reliable workers? How much herbicide can it eliminate? Does the crop perform better without chemical weed control? Can one operator supervise several robots at the same time? And perhaps most importantly: will the machine reliably perform during the short periods when every hour counts?

These questions make calculating a business case more complicated than ever. They also make it much harder to identify the "best buy". A robot that makes little financial sense on one farm may deliver an excellent return on another. Some manufacturers openly acknowledge this reality. During the same event, Bas Andela,founder of Dutch manufacturer Andela Techniek, summed it up perfectly: "Don't ask what the robot costs. Ask what it earns the grower."

What are robots really competing against?

Perhaps that is the more interesting question. Today, many growers still compare a weeding robot with a sprayer, a mechanical hoe or seasonal labour. But what happens if more herbicides disappear from the market? Or if labour shortages continue to worsen? The comparison changes completely. The robot no longer has to compete with chemical weed control on price. Instead, it may become one of the few practical solutions left. That changes the entire pricing discussion.

Agricultural robots will undoubtedly become faster, smarter and more reliable over the next decade. But that does not automatically mean they will become cheaper. Just as a 300 hp tractor is not priced solely according to the amount of steel it contains, robots will increasingly be valued according to the economic benefits they deliver on the farm.

There is, however, one important difference. Many farmers have a strong emotional connection with a particular tractor brand. Years of experience, family history, dealer relationships and brand loyalty all influence purchasing decisions. Just as in the automotive industry, customers are often willing to pay more for the brand they believe in. I'm not sure the same will ever happen with agricultural robots. Farmers may still fall in love with the colour of a tractor's bonnet. But when it comes to robots, I suspect they’ll fall in love with the business case instead.
